Reduced physical damage from hitting something is not the RF-design purpose being tested.

G4E03.hint: A 100-watt HF mobile rig wants short, direct, fused leads with low voltage drop. That means straight to the battery with heavy wire.
G4E03.explanation: The correct answer is "To the battery using heavy-gauge wire" because the battery is the proper high-current DC source in the vehicle, and heavy-gauge wire minimizes voltage drop and heating. Fusing the leads protects the installation.
G4E03.mnemonic: HF mobile: battery, big wire, fuse.
why_wrong: Connecting directly to the alternator or generator is not the standard best-practice power source for the radio.
Balanced transmission line is feed line, not appropriate DC power wiring.
This combines the wrong power source with the wrong type of cable.

G4E04.hint: Accessory sockets are often wired for lighter loads than a 100-watt HF rig needs. The issue is current capacity and voltage drop.
G4E04.explanation: The correct answer is "The socket’s wiring may be inadequate for the current drawn by the transceiver" because a 100-watt HF transceiver can draw substantial current on transmit. Many auxiliary sockets use smaller wire and connectors that cannot support that load reliably.
why_wrong: RF shielding is not the main reason this socket is a poor choice.
Modern vehicle accessory sockets are not generally rejected because of reversed polarity.
The engine overheating claim is unrelated to the real electrical limitation here.

G4E05.hint: In HF mobile, the big compromise is almost always the tiny antenna. Short mobile antennas work, but efficiency is the main limiter.
G4E05.explanation: The correct answer is "Efficiency of the electrically short antenna" because mobile HF antennas are usually much shorter than a full quarter-wave. That makes them lossy and less efficient, which is often the biggest limitation in mobile HF performance.
why_wrong: Picket fencing is more of a VHF/UHF mobile propagation term, not the main HF mobile limitation.
Power wire gauge matters, but it is not usually the biggest overall limit on HF mobile performance.
FCC rules do not impose the kind of mobile-only HF output restriction suggested here.

G4E06.hint: Shortened antennas often have higher Q, which means a narrower usable frequency range. The clue is limited bandwidth.
G4E06.explanation: The correct answer is "Operating bandwidth may be very limited" because shortened mobile antennas often have high Q and require careful tuning over a narrow frequency range. They may work well only across a small part of the band before retuning is needed.
why_wrong: Short antennas do not inherently distort transmitted signals just because they are short.
The Q is often relatively high, not very low.
A shortened antenna does not inherently cause more harmonic radiation.

G4E07.hint: Modern vehicles are full of possible noise sources: charging systems, fuel pumps, and computers. When you see several plausible automotive noise generators, expect 'all of these.'
G4E07.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because vehicle electrical systems contain many potential RF noise sources. Alternators and charging systems, fuel delivery components, and onboard control computers can all radiate or conduct interference into an HF mobile setup.
why_wrong: This is a real interference source, but not the only one listed.
This can also produce noise, but it is still only part of the full answer.
Control electronics are another valid source, which is why the complete answer includes all of them.

G4E08.hint: Solar panels use a mix of series and parallel cell connections so the panel can build both usable voltage and current. That is the key idea.
G4E08.explanation: The correct answer is "Series-parallel" because photovoltaic panels combine cells in series to raise voltage and in parallel groupings to increase current capability. Practical panels use both arrangements together.
G4E08.mnemonic: Solar cells stack for volts, team for amps.
why_wrong: Shunt is not the normal cell interconnection scheme for the whole panel.
Bypass diodes may be used in panels, but the cells themselves are not simply connected in 'bypass' configuration.
A full-wave bridge is a rectifier arrangement, not a photovoltaic cell layout.

G4E09.hint: The memorization number for a single illuminated silicon photovoltaic cell is about half a volt open-circuit.
G4E09.explanation: The correct answer is "0.5 VDC" because a typical silicon photovoltaic cell produces roughly half a volt with no load attached under full illumination. That is why many cells are placed in series to build usable panel voltage.
G4E09.mnemonic: One silicon cell, about half a volt.
why_wrong: 0.02 volts is far too low for a normal illuminated silicon cell.
0.2 volts is below the usual approximate open-circuit value.
1.38 volts is too high for a single silicon photovoltaic cell.

G4E10.hint: A series diode is there mainly as a one-way valve so the battery cannot feed backward into the panel when sunlight is weak or absent.
G4E10.explanation: The correct answer is "To prevent discharge of the battery through the panel during times of low or no illumination" because a diode allows current to flow from panel to battery while blocking reverse current from battery to panel. Without it, the battery could slowly discharge backward through the panel.
G4E10.mnemonic: Solar diode = no backflow at night.
why_wrong: A simple series diode is not a full charging regulator.
Current limiting requires more than just the diode's one-way function.
Overvoltage protection is the job of proper charge control, not just the series blocking diode.

G4E11.hint: Lithium iron phosphate batteries need controlled charging. The key precaution is using a proper charge controller between panel and battery.
G4E11.explanation: The correct answer is "The solar panel must have a charge controller" because LiFePO4 batteries require correct charging voltage and current management. A charge controller protects the battery and ensures safe, proper charging behavior from the solar panel.
why_wrong: Grounding the frame may be good practice in some systems, but it is not the key battery-protection precaution asked here.
Battery orientation is not the relevant issue for this chemistry and question.
A series resistor is not an acceptable substitute for proper charge control.

G5A01.hint: In a series LC circuit at resonance, inductive reactance and capacitive reactance cancel, leaving very little opposition except resistance. Series resonance means low impedance.
G5A01.explanation: The correct answer is "Resonance causes impedance to be very low" because when XL equals XC in a series LC circuit, the two reactances cancel each other. The circuit is then limited mainly by its small resistive losses, so total impedance drops sharply.
why_wrong: Very high impedance is what you would associate with parallel resonance, not series resonance.
There is no geometric-mean rule like this for the impedance of a resonant LC circuit.
There is no arithmetic-mean rule here either; the key idea is reactance cancellation.

G5A02.hint: Reactance is AC-only opposition caused by energy storage in capacitors or inductors. If the answer mentions AC plus capacitance or inductance, you are in the right place.
G5A02.explanation: The correct answer is "Opposition to the flow of alternating current caused by capacitance or inductance" because reactance comes from capacitors and inductors resisting changes in voltage or current in AC circuits. It is different from plain resistance, which applies to both AC and DC.
G5A02.mnemonic: Reactance resists AC through L or C.
why_wrong: That describes ordinary resistance in a DC context, not reactance.
Reactance opposes current flow; it does not reinforce it.
Again, reactance is opposition, not reinforcement.

G5A03.hint: An inductor opposes AC with reactance. The question is asking for the general AC-opposition term, not a special inductor-only new word.
G5A03.explanation: The correct answer is "Reactance" because the opposition an inductor presents to alternating current is called inductive reactance. The broader category name is still reactance.
why_wrong: Conductance is the opposite of resistance, not the inductor's AC opposition term.
Reluctance belongs to magnetic circuits, not this AC current-flow definition.
Admittance is the inverse of impedance, not specifically an inductor's opposition to AC.

G5A04.hint: A capacitor also opposes AC with reactance. Same core term as the inductor question, just capacitive instead of inductive.
G5A04.explanation: The correct answer is "Reactance" because the opposition a capacitor presents to alternating current is called capacitive reactance. The shared category name is reactance.
G5A04.mnemonic: Capacitor blocks AC with X too.
why_wrong: Conductance is not the capacitor's opposition-to-AC term.
Reluctance is for magnetic paths, not capacitor AC behavior.
Admittance is an inverse quantity, not the name of the capacitor's AC opposition.

G5A05.hint: For inductors, frequency and reactance rise together. Higher frequency means the inductor fights the current more.
G5A05.explanation: The correct answer is "As the frequency of the applied AC increases, the reactance increases" because inductive reactance follows XL = 2pifL. As frequency goes up, XL goes up too.
why_wrong: This has the frequency relationship backward for an inductor.
Reactance depends on frequency and component value, not directly on AC amplitude.
Again, amplitude is the wrong variable here.

G5A06.hint: Capacitors do the opposite of inductors: as frequency rises, capacitive reactance falls.
G5A06.explanation: The correct answer is "As the frequency of the applied AC increases, the reactance decreases" because capacitive reactance follows XC = 1 / (2pifC). Increasing frequency makes the denominator larger, so XC gets smaller.
why_wrong: That would be true for an inductor, not a capacitor.
Amplitude is not what determines capacitive reactance here.
Again, reactance is changing with frequency, not amplitude.

G5A07.hint: If impedance is opposition to current flow, its inverse is willingness to let current flow. That inverse is admittance.
G5A07.explanation: The correct answer is "Admittance" because admittance is mathematically the reciprocal of impedance. It represents how easily AC can flow through a circuit.
why_wrong: Conductance is the real part of admittance, not the whole inverse of impedance.
Susceptance is the reactive part of admittance, not the full quantity.
Reluctance belongs to magnetic circuits, not the inverse of impedance.

G5A08.hint: Impedance is the AC version of opposition, and by Ohm's law form it is V divided by I.
G5A08.explanation: The correct answer is "The ratio of voltage to current" because impedance is defined as Z = V / I in AC circuits. It plays the same role in AC that resistance plays in simple DC circuits, though impedance can include reactance as well as resistance.
why_wrong: Current divided by voltage would be the inverse relationship, closer to admittance.
The product of voltage and current is power, not impedance.
Multiplying current by reactance does not define impedance.

G5A09.hint: Reactance is measured in the same unit as resistance because both describe opposition to current: ohms.
G5A09.explanation: The correct answer is "Ohm" because reactance, like resistance and impedance, is measured in ohms. Even though reactance comes from capacitance or inductance, its unit is still the ohm.
G5A09.mnemonic: Resistance, reactance, impedance: all in ohms.
why_wrong: Farad is the unit of capacitance, not reactance.
Ampere measures current, not opposition to current.
Siemens is the unit of conductance or admittance, not reactance.

G5A10.hint: At RF, matching can be done in several ways: transformers, matching networks, and even transmission-line sections. When all listed methods are valid, pick 'all of these.'
G5A10.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because RF impedance matching can be accomplished with transformers, Pi-networks, and appropriately chosen lengths of transmission line. Each is a legitimate matching method depending on the application.
G5A10.mnemonic: Many RF paths can make a match.
why_wrong: A transformer can match impedance, but it is not the only valid device listed.
A Pi-network also works, but the question includes more than one correct method.
Transmission-line sections can match too, which is why the complete answer is all of them.

G5A11.hint: The letter for reactance is X. Keep the common symbols straight: R for resistance, X for reactance, Z for impedance, Y for admittance.
G5A11.explanation: The correct answer is "X" because reactance is conventionally represented by the symbol X in circuit notation and formulas.
why_wrong: Z is the symbol for impedance, not reactance.
B is commonly used for susceptance, not reactance.
Y is the symbol for admittance, not reactance.

G5A12.hint: Resonance in an LC circuit happens when the inductor's reactance and the capacitor's reactance are equal and opposite, so they cancel.
G5A12.explanation: The correct answer is "Inductive reactance and capacitive reactance cancel" because at resonance XL equals XC. Their opposite signs in the circuit mean the reactive parts cancel, leaving only the resistive portion.
G5A12.mnemonic: At resonance, XL and XC erase each other.
why_wrong: Current and voltage are not simply made equal by resonance.
Resistance is not cancelled in a normal resonant LC circuit.
The circuit does not automatically radiate all its energy as radio waves.

G5B01.hint: The standard shortcut is simple: doubling or halving power is about 3 dB. Keep `2x power = 3 dB` memorized cold.
G5B01.explanation: The correct answer is "Approximately 3 dB" because a factor-of-two change in power corresponds to 10 log10(2), which is about 3 dB. On the exam, doubling power and 3 dB are a fixed pair.
why_wrong: 2 dB is too small for a doubling of power.
6 dB corresponds to about four times the power, not two times.
9 dB is much larger than a factor-of-two power change.

G5B02.hint: In a parallel circuit, branch currents add together at the source. Think Kirchhoff: current splits, then the totals add.
G5B02.explanation: The correct answer is "It equals the sum of the currents through each branch" because total current entering a parallel network is the sum of the branch currents. Each branch takes its share, and the source supplies all of them together.
why_wrong: The total is not the average; it is the sum.
Adding branches usually increases the total current drawn from the source if voltage stays the same.
Reciprocals are used in some resistance calculations, not in stating total current this way.

G5B03.hint: Use `P = V² / R` when voltage and resistance are given. Here that is `400² / 800 = 160000 / 800 = 200` watts.
G5B03.explanation: The correct answer is "200 watts" because with 400 VDC across an 800-ohm load, power is P = V squared divided by R. That gives 400 x 400 / 800 = 200 watts.
G5B03.mnemonic: Given V and R, square V first.
why_wrong: 0.5 watts would come from using the wrong relationship entirely.
400 watts is what you get if you skip the division step or misuse the formula.
3200 watts is far too high for these numbers and reflects a formula mistake.

G5B04.hint: Use `P = V x I`. Here `12 x 0.2 = 2.4` watts. Watch the decimal point.
G5B04.explanation: The correct answer is "2.4 watts" because electrical power is voltage times current. With 12 volts and 0.2 amperes, power is 2.4 watts.
why_wrong: 24 watts is what you get if you forget the decimal in 0.2 A.
6 watts does not match the simple P = VI calculation.
60 watts is far too large and reflects a decimal or formula error.

G5B05.hint: With current and resistance, use `P = I²R`. Convert `7.0 mA` to `0.007 A` first, then square it.
G5B05.explanation: The correct answer is "Approximately 61 milliwatts" because P = I squared times R. Using 0.007 A gives 0.007² x 1250 = 0.06125 W, which is about 61 milliwatts.
G5B05.mnemonic: Milliamps must become amps before squaring.
why_wrong: 61 watts ignores the milliamps-to-amps conversion and is far too large.
11 milliwatts does not match the correct I²R calculation.
11 watts is also much too large and reflects a unit-conversion mistake.

G5B06.hint: Convert `Vpp` to `Vpeak` by dividing by 2, then use `PEP = Vpeak² / (2R)`. For 200 Vpp into 50 ohms, that gives 100 watts.
G5B06.explanation: The correct answer is "100 watts" because 200 volts peak-to-peak means 100 volts peak. For a sine wave, PEP into a resistive load is Vpeak squared divided by 2R, so 100² / (2 x 50) = 100 watts.
why_wrong: 1.4 watts is far too low for 200 Vpp across 50 ohms.
353.5 watts usually comes from mixing up RMS and peak relationships.
400 watts is what you might get by using the wrong voltage form directly.

G5B07.hint: RMS is the 'heating equivalent' value. If an AC value gives the same resistor heating as DC, it is the RMS value.
G5B07.explanation: The correct answer is "The RMS value" because RMS voltage or current is defined so that it produces the same power dissipation in a resistor as a DC quantity of the same value.
why_wrong: Peak-to-peak does not describe equivalent heating power in a resistor.
Peak value is not the same as the DC-equivalent heating value.
The reciprocal of RMS has no meaning here.

G5B08.hint: For a sine wave, `Vpeak = 1.414 x Vrms`, then `Vpp = 2 x Vpeak`. So `120 x 1.414 x 2 = 339.4` volts.
G5B08.explanation: The correct answer is "339.4 volts" because 120 Vrms corresponds to about 169.7 Vpeak, and doubling that gives 339.4 Vpp.
G5B08.mnemonic: RMS to peak, then double for peak-to-peak.
why_wrong: 84.8 volts is below even the RMS value, so it cannot be the peak-to-peak value.
169.7 volts is the peak value, not peak-to-peak.
240 volts is just twice the RMS value, which is not the correct sine-wave conversion.

G5B09.hint: For a sine wave, `Vrms = Vpeak / 1.414`. So `17 / 1.414` is about 12 volts.
G5B09.explanation: The correct answer is "12 volts" because the RMS value of a sine wave is the peak value divided by the square root of 2. Using 17 volts peak gives approximately 12 volts RMS.
why_wrong: 8.5 volts would be half the peak value, not the RMS value.
24 volts is greater than the peak value, so it cannot be the RMS value.
34 volts is just double the peak value and is clearly incorrect.

G5B10.hint: A 1 dB power loss leaves about 79.4 percent of the power, so the loss itself is about 20.6 percent. This is a memorization-number question.
G5B10.explanation: The correct answer is "20.6 percent" because a 1 dB loss corresponds to a power ratio of about 0.794. That means about 79.4 percent remains and about 20.6 percent is lost.
why_wrong: 10.9 percent is too small for a full 1 dB loss.
12.2 percent is also too small.
25.9 percent is too large.

G5B11.hint: For an unmodulated carrier, PEP and average power are the same. No modulation envelope means no higher peaks than the steady carrier.
G5B11.explanation: The correct answer is "1.00" because an unmodulated carrier has constant amplitude. Since the peak envelope power never rises above the average power, their ratio is exactly 1.
why_wrong: 0.707 is a sine-wave voltage conversion factor, not this power ratio.
1.414 is another voltage conversion factor, not the PEP-to-average ratio for a steady carrier.
2.00 would imply peaks twice the average, which is not true for an unmodulated carrier.

G5B12.hint: Use `P = V² / R`, so `V = sqrt(PR)`. Here `sqrt(1200 x 50) = sqrt(60000)`, which is about 245 volts RMS.
G5B12.explanation: The correct answer is "245 volts" because with a 50-ohm load dissipating 1200 watts, RMS voltage is the square root of power times resistance. The square root of 60,000 is about 245.
G5B12.mnemonic: Given P and R, root PR for V.
why_wrong: 173 volts is too low for 1200 watts across 50 ohms.
346 volts is too high and suggests using the wrong voltage form.
692 volts is much too high for this load and power.

G5B13.hint: For an unmodulated carrier, average power equals PEP. So if average power is 1060 watts, PEP is also 1060 watts.
G5B13.explanation: The correct answer is "1060 watts" because an unmodulated carrier has constant amplitude and therefore its peak envelope power is the same as its average power.
why_wrong: 530 watts would be half the average power, which is not correct here.
1500 watts is higher than the steady carrier's actual PEP.
2120 watts doubles the value for no reason; unmodulated carrier PEP does not double average power.

G5B14.hint: Convert `500 Vpp` to `250 Vpeak`, then use `PEP = Vpeak² / (2R)`. That gives `250² / 100 = 625` watts.
G5B14.explanation: The correct answer is "625 watts" because a 500-volt peak-to-peak sine wave has a peak voltage of 250 volts. PEP into 50 ohms is then 250 squared divided by 100, which equals 625 watts.
why_wrong: 8.75 watts is far too small for 500 Vpp across 50 ohms.
2500 watts comes from using the wrong voltage term in the formula.
5000 watts is even farther off and reflects skipping multiple conversion steps.

G5C01.hint: Transformers work because changing current in one coil creates a changing magnetic field that induces voltage in the other. That is mutual inductance.
G5C01.explanation: The correct answer is "Mutual inductance" because the primary winding's alternating current creates a changing magnetic field, and that changing field induces a voltage in the secondary winding. That transformer action is the definition of mutual inductance.
why_wrong: Capacitive coupling is not the normal operating principle of a transformer.
Displacement current is not the transformer effect being tested here.
Mutual capacitance is not the main mechanism for voltage transfer in a transformer.

G5C02.hint: If you drive a 4:1 step-down transformer backward, it becomes a 1:4 step-up transformer. Backward means the voltage change flips too.
G5C02.explanation: The correct answer is "The input voltage is multiplied by 4" because applying the signal to the secondary of a 4-to-1 step-down transformer reverses its action. What used to step down by 4 now steps up by 4.
G5C02.mnemonic: Run a transformer backward, reverse the ratio.
why_wrong: That would describe normal step-down use, not reversed operation.
No extra resistance is required just because the transformer is used in reverse.
Parallel resistance is also not the point of this question.

G5C03.hint: Parallel resistance is always less than the smallest resistor. Since the smallest here is 10 ohms, the answer must be below 10 ohms, which quickly points to 5.9 ohms.
G5C03.explanation: The correct answer is "5.9 ohms" because for parallel resistors you add reciprocals: 1/10 + 1/20 + 1/50 = 0.17, and 1/0.17 is about 5.9 ohms.
why_wrong: 0.17 is the reciprocal sum before the final inversion, not the resistance itself.
17 ohms is above the smallest resistor, so it cannot be the parallel total.
80 ohms would be the series sum, not the parallel result.

G5C04.hint: Two resistors in parallel must total less than the smallest one, so it has to be below 100 ohms. The quick result for 100 || 200 is about 67 ohms.
G5C04.explanation: The correct answer is "67 ohms" because 1/R = 1/100 + 1/200 = 0.015, so R is about 66.7 ohms. Rounding gives 67 ohms.
G5C04.mnemonic: 100 in parallel with 200 gives about two-thirds of 100.
why_wrong: 300 ohms is the series sum, not the parallel result.
150 ohms is still above the smaller resistor, so it cannot be right for a parallel pair.
75 ohms is close, but the actual reciprocal calculation gives about 67 ohms.

G5C05.hint: In a step-up transformer, the primary is the low-voltage side, so it carries more current. More current needs thicker wire.
G5C05.explanation: The correct answer is "To accommodate the higher current of the primary" because in a voltage step-up transformer, the primary side operates at lower voltage and therefore higher current for roughly the same power. Thicker wire helps carry that current safely.
G5C05.mnemonic: Lower volts side, larger current side.
why_wrong: Wire size is not primarily chosen to improve magnetic coupling.
Parasitic oscillation prevention is not the main reason for the larger primary wire.
Equal winding volume is not the design rule being tested.

G5C06.hint: Transformer voltage ratio follows turns ratio. `1500 / 500 = 3`, so the secondary voltage is `120 x 3 = 360` volts.
G5C06.explanation: The correct answer is "360 volts" because a transformer with three times as many turns on the secondary as the primary steps voltage up by a factor of three. Applying 120 VAC therefore produces 360 volts on the secondary.
why_wrong: 120 volts would be no change, but this is a 3-to-1 step-up ratio.
40 volts is the wrong direction for the ratio; that would be stepping down.
25.5 volts is unrelated to the turns ratio here.

G5C07.hint: Impedance ratio uses the square of turns ratio, so turns ratio is the square root of `600 / 50 = 12`. The square root of 12 is about 3.5.
G5C07.explanation: The correct answer is "3.5 to 1" because matching 600 ohms to 50 ohms requires an impedance ratio of 12 to 1. The transformer turns ratio is the square root of the impedance ratio, and sqrt(12) is about 3.46, which rounds to 3.5 to 1.
G5C07.mnemonic: Turns ratio is root of impedance ratio.
why_wrong: 12 to 1 is the impedance ratio, not the turns ratio.
24 to 1 is much too large.
144 to 1 comes from squaring when you should have taken the square root.

G5C08.hint: Capacitors in parallel add directly. Convert `750 pF` to `0.750 nF`, then add `5 + 5 + 0.750 = 10.750 nF`.
G5C08.explanation: The correct answer is "10.750 nanofarads" because parallel capacitances simply add. After converting 750 picofarads to 0.750 nanofarads, the total becomes 10.750 nanofarads.
why_wrong: 576.9 nanofarads is wildly too large for these component values.
1,733 picofarads is too small because the two 5.0 nF capacitors already total 10,000 pF.
3,583 picofarads is also too small for the same reason.

G5C09.hint: Equal capacitors in series divide by the number of capacitors. Three `100 uF` capacitors in series give `100 / 3 = 33.3 uF`.
G5C09.explanation: The correct answer is "33.3 microfarads" because capacitors in series produce a smaller total capacitance, and for equal values you can divide one capacitor value by the number of capacitors.
why_wrong: 0.33 microfarads is much too small; the series total is reduced, but not by that much.
3.0 microfarads is also far too small.
300 microfarads would be the sum in parallel, not in series.

G5C10.hint: Inductors in parallel behave like resistors in parallel. Three equal `10 mH` inductors in parallel give `10 / 3 = 3.3 mH`.
G5C10.explanation: The correct answer is "3.3 millihenries" because equal inductors in parallel divide by the number of branches, just like equal resistors in parallel.
why_wrong: 0.30 henries is 300 millihenries, much too large.
3.3 henries is 1000 times too large.
30 millihenries would be a series-type increase, not a parallel reduction.

G5C11.hint: Inductors in series add directly. `20 mH + 50 mH = 70 mH` with no extra trick.
G5C11.explanation: The correct answer is "70 millihenries" because series inductances add just like series resistances when there is no special coupling information given.
why_wrong: 7 millihenries is too low and reflects a wrong parallel-style idea.
14.3 millihenries comes from a reciprocal method that belongs to parallel combinations.
1,000 millihenries is much too large for 20 plus 50 millihenries.

G5C12.hint: For two capacitors in series, use product over sum: `(20 x 50) / (20 + 50) = 1000 / 70 = 14.3 uF`.
G5C12.explanation: The correct answer is "14.3 microfarads" because series capacitance for two capacitors is C1 x C2 divided by C1 + C2. That gives 14.3 microfarads here.
G5C12.mnemonic: Two caps in series: product over sum.
why_wrong: 0.07 microfarads is far too small for 20 and 50 microfarads in series.
70 microfarads would be the sum, which is not how series capacitors combine.
1,000 microfarads is the product before dividing by the sum, not the final answer.

G5C13.hint: To increase capacitance, add more capacitance in a way that makes values add. Capacitors add in parallel.
G5C13.explanation: The correct answer is "A capacitor in parallel" because parallel capacitors add directly, increasing the total capacitance.
why_wrong: An inductor changes a different property entirely.
An inductor in parallel still does not directly increase capacitance.
A capacitor in series lowers total capacitance instead of increasing it.

G5C14.hint: To increase inductance, add more inductance in the configuration where values add directly. Inductors add in series.
G5C14.explanation: The correct answer is "An inductor in series" because inductances in series add directly, increasing the total inductance.
why_wrong: A capacitor changes capacitance, not inductance.
A capacitor in parallel still does not increase inductance.
An inductor in parallel reduces total inductance instead of increasing it.

G6A01.hint: For a 12-volt lead-acid battery, the memorization number for the lowest discharge voltage that still protects battery life is 10.5 volts.
G6A01.explanation: The correct answer is "10.5 volts" because discharging a standard 12-volt lead-acid battery much below that level can shorten its life. This is the classic exam threshold to remember.
why_wrong: 6 volts is far too low and would represent severe overdischarge.
8.5 volts is also too low for preserving maximum battery life.
12 volts is nominal battery voltage, not the minimum allowable discharge value.

G6A02.hint: Low internal resistance means the battery wastes less voltage inside itself, so it can deliver heavier current to the load.
G6A02.explanation: The correct answer is "High discharge current" because a battery with low internal resistance can supply large current with less internal voltage drop and heating. That is especially useful for high-current loads.
G6A02.mnemonic: Low internal resistance, high current delivery.
why_wrong: Low internal resistance does not automatically guarantee long life.
Battery voltage is set by chemistry and cell count, not just internal resistance.
Recharge speed depends on charging system and battery design, not simply low internal resistance.

G6A03.hint: Germanium diodes have the lower forward drop. The standard pair is germanium about 0.3 V, silicon about 0.7 V.
G6A03.explanation: The correct answer is "0.3 volts" because germanium diodes typically begin conducting with a forward threshold of about 0.3 V, noticeably lower than silicon diodes.
why_wrong: 0.1 volt is too low for the usual germanium-diode threshold value taught on the exam.
0.7 volts is the typical silicon junction diode threshold.
1.0 volt is too high for a standard germanium diode.

G6A04.hint: Electrolytics are the go-to parts when you need a lot of capacitance in a small package. That is their signature trait.
G6A04.explanation: The correct answer is "High capacitance for a given volume" because electrolytic capacitors are designed to provide relatively large capacitance values compared with many other capacitor types of similar size.
G6A04.mnemonic: Electrolytic = lots of microfarads in little space.
why_wrong: Electrolytics are not known for especially tight tolerance.
They usually have more leakage than many other capacitor types, not less.
Electrolytics are generally not the preferred capacitor type for RF service.

G6A05.hint: Silicon diodes use the higher common threshold. Keep the exam pair straight: germanium 0.3 V, silicon 0.7 V.
G6A05.explanation: The correct answer is "0.7 volts" because a typical silicon junction diode has a forward threshold of about 0.7 V before significant conduction begins.
why_wrong: 0.1 volt is too low for a standard silicon junction diode.
0.3 volts is the value more commonly associated with germanium.
1.0 volt is higher than the usual textbook threshold.

G6A06.hint: Wire-wound resistors are literally little coils of wire, so at RF they can behave inductively instead of like plain resistors.
G6A06.explanation: The correct answer is "The resistor’s inductance could make circuit performance unpredictable" because the wound wire adds inductance. In RF circuits that stray inductance can detune or alter circuit behavior.
G6A06.mnemonic: Wire-wound means wire-coil, and coil means inductance.
why_wrong: Tolerance is not the main RF problem with wire-wound resistors.
Overheating is not the special reason they are avoided in RF circuits.
Stray capacitance can exist in many parts, but the classic issue with wire-wound resistors is inductance.

G6A07.hint: A transistor used as a switch should be either fully off or fully on. In BJT language that means cutoff and saturation.
G6A07.explanation: The correct answer is "Saturation and cutoff" because a switching transistor is operated at the two extremes: cutoff for OFF and saturation for ON. The active region is for amplification, not ideal switching.
G6A07.mnemonic: Switches snap between cutoff and saturation.
why_wrong: The active region is where a transistor behaves like an amplifier, not a simple switch.
Peak and valley current points are not the standard BJT switching terms.
Enhancement and depletion describe FET types or modes, not BJT switch operating points.

G6A08.hint: Low-voltage ceramic capacitors are common, cheap general-purpose parts. The exam clue is cost, not precision or stability.
G6A08.explanation: The correct answer is "Comparatively low cost" because low-voltage ceramic capacitors are widely manufactured and inexpensive. They are popular where low price matters more than premium tolerance or stability.
why_wrong: Ceramics are not generally chosen because of especially tight tolerance in this context.
High stability is more associated with specialized capacitor types.
Very high capacitance per volume is more the strength of electrolytics than small ceramic caps.

G6A09.hint: The `MOS` in MOSFET includes `oxide`, which is the insulating layer between gate and channel. Insulated gate is the signature clue.
G6A09.explanation: The correct answer is "The gate is separated from the channel by a thin insulating layer" because a MOSFET uses an insulated gate structure. The gate controls the channel electrically without direct conductive contact.
G6A09.mnemonic: MOSFET means metal-oxide-semiconductor, so think insulated gate.
why_wrong: A back-biased junction describes JFET behavior more than MOSFET construction.
The source and drain are not separated from each other by the thin gate-insulating layer being described.
This does not describe the defining MOSFET gate structure.

G6A10.hint: In a vacuum tube, the element that actually controls electron flow from cathode to plate is the control grid. Its name says its job.
G6A10.explanation: The correct answer is "Control grid" because the control grid varies how many electrons are allowed to travel from cathode to plate, making it the main regulating electrode.
why_wrong: The suppressor grid has a different purpose, mainly reducing secondary-emission effects.
The screen grid shields and improves performance, but it is not the main control element.
Trigger electrode is not the standard vacuum-tube element doing this job.

G6A11.hint: Above self-resonance, an inductor stops acting mainly like an inductor because its stray capacitance takes over. Above SRF, it looks capacitive.
G6A11.explanation: The correct answer is "It becomes capacitive" because every real inductor has stray capacitance. Above the self-resonant frequency, that capacitance dominates the behavior and the component acts more like a capacitor than an inductor.
G6A11.mnemonic: Above self-resonance, the inductor switches sides.
why_wrong: Its reactance does not simply keep increasing as if it remained a pure inductor.
Self-resonance does not inherently mean the inductor starts generating harmonics.
Operation above self-resonance is undesirable for circuit behavior, but it does not automatically mean catastrophic failure.

G6A12.hint: The screen grid acts like an electrostatic shield between control grid and plate. The exam clue is reduced grid-to-plate capacitance.
G6A12.explanation: The correct answer is "To reduce grid-to-plate capacitance" because the screen grid helps isolate the control grid from the plate. That shielding improves high-frequency performance and reduces unwanted feedback.
why_wrong: Efficiency may be affected by tube design, but this is not the primary screen-grid purpose asked here.
The screen grid does not exist to increase control-grid resistance.
Decreasing plate resistance is not the standard textbook answer for the screen grid's primary purpose.

G6B01.hint: With ferrites, the important variable is the material mix. Different mixes are made to work best over different frequency ranges.
G6B01.explanation: The correct answer is "The composition, or “mix,” of materials used" because ferrite performance depends strongly on the magnetic material formulation. Different mixes give different impedance and loss characteristics at different frequencies.
why_wrong: Conductivity is not the main design factor that sets ferrite frequency behavior.
Physical thickness alone does not determine the frequency-performance family of a ferrite core.
Core geometry matters in use, but the mix is what primarily sets frequency performance.

G6B02.hint: MMIC is one of those direct expansion questions. The key middle word to remember is microwave.
G6B02.explanation: The correct answer is "Monolithic Microwave Integrated Circuit" because MMIC is the standard abbreviation for an integrated microwave-frequency circuit fabricated as a single chip.
why_wrong: Multi-mode is not what the first M stands for here.
Metal monolayer is not the expansion of MMIC.
Mode modulated is also unrelated to the standard term.

G6B03.hint: CMOS logic is famous for sipping power compared with TTL, especially when not switching much. Power savings is the big exam clue.
G6B03.explanation: The correct answer is "Low power consumption" because CMOS circuits generally draw much less static power than TTL circuits. That low power use is one of CMOS's classic advantages.
why_wrong: CMOS is not chosen because it handles especially high power.
RF amplification is not the main comparison point in CMOS versus TTL logic families.
Power-supply regulation is not the key advantage being tested.

G6B04.hint: For the exam, the memorized upper limit for low-SWR BNC use is about 4 GHz. It is much higher than the VHF-only intuition many people have.
G6B04.explanation: The correct answer is "4 GHz" because BNC connectors can still be used with acceptably low SWR into the low-gigahertz range under proper conditions. The pool's memorization number is 4 GHz.
why_wrong: 50 MHz is far below the typical upper limit asked here.
500 MHz is still lower than the pool's stated value.
40 GHz is much too high for ordinary BNC low-SWR use.

G6B05.hint: Toroidal ferrite inductors pack several advantages together: high inductance, controlled ferrite properties, and magnetic field containment. That combination points to 'all of these.'
G6B05.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because ferrite toroids can provide large inductance values, use a core optimized for a frequency range, and confine most of the magnetic field inside the core.
why_wrong: This is true, but it is only one of several advantages listed.
This is also true, but not the complete answer.
This is true too, which is why the full answer includes all the listed choices.

G6B06.hint: An op-amp is an analog building block used to process continuously varying voltages, not digital logic states.
G6B06.explanation: The correct answer is "Analog" because an operational amplifier is designed to amplify and manipulate analog voltage signals in circuits such as filters, amplifiers, and active control stages.
why_wrong: Digital devices process logic states rather than the continuous analog behavior typical of op-amps.
MMIC is a different category of microwave integrated circuit.
Programmable logic is also a digital category, not what an op-amp is.

G6B07.hint: Type N connectors are rugged RF connectors known for weather resistance and good microwave performance. Think outdoor RF, not small snap-on data connectors.
G6B07.explanation: The correct answer is "A moisture-resistant RF connector useful to 10 GHz" because the Type N connector is designed for RF work, including outdoor or weather-exposed use, and performs well into the gigahertz range.
G6B07.mnemonic: Type N = nice outdoors, near microwave.
why_wrong: A small bayonet RF connector describes a BNC-style idea, not Type N.
Low noise figure is not how connector types are categorized here.
Type N is not just a plated PL-259 variant.

G6B08.hint: LEDs are diodes that light when current flows in the normal conducting direction. Light means forward bias.
G6B08.explanation: The correct answer is "Forward biased" because an LED emits light when current passes through its junction in the forward direction. Reverse bias does not produce normal light emission.
why_wrong: Tunnel-effect region is unrelated to normal LED operation.
Zener voltage refers to reverse-breakdown behavior of Zener diodes, not ordinary LED emission.
Reverse bias normally prevents the LED from lighting.

G6B10.hint: Ferrite beads fight common-mode current by making that unwanted current path harder to flow through. The key word is impedance.
G6B10.explanation: The correct answer is "By creating an impedance in the current’s path" because a ferrite bead or core increases the impedance seen by common-mode RF current on the cable exterior, reducing that unwanted current.
why_wrong: A ferrite does not convert common-mode current into differential-mode current.
It also does not inject a cancelling current like an active circuit would.
Ferrites do not 'expel' magnetic fields; they modify the impedance of the path.

G6B11.hint: SMA connectors are the small threaded microwave connectors you see on compact RF gear. Small plus threaded plus GHz is the giveaway.
G6B11.explanation: The correct answer is "A small threaded connector suitable for signals up to several GHz" because SMA connectors are compact RF connectors with threaded coupling and excellent performance at high frequencies.
why_wrong: SMA is not an adapter designation.
Serial multiple access is unrelated to the connector name.
SMA is threaded, not a push-on high-voltage connector.

G6B12.hint: For low-frequency or DC accessory-style connections, RCA phono plugs are common. The other listed connectors are more RF-oriented.
G6B12.explanation: The correct answer is "RCA Phono" because RCA connectors are commonly used for lower-frequency signal and audio-style interconnections, including some low-frequency or DC connections on equipment.
why_wrong: PL-259 is mainly an RF coax connector, not the common low-frequency/DC answer here.
BNC is also primarily an RF/data-style coax connector.
Type N is another RF connector, usually used where better high-frequency performance is needed.

G7A01.hint: A bleeder resistor is a safety-and-drain part. Its job is to bleed stored charge off the power-supply filter capacitors after shutdown.
G7A01.explanation: The correct answer is "It discharges the filter capacitors when power is removed" because bleeder resistors provide a path for stored energy in high-voltage filter capacitors to drain away after the supply is turned off.
why_wrong: A bleeder resistor is not a fuse.
Reducing shock hazard is a benefit, but it does so specifically by discharging filter capacitors.
Ground-loop current is unrelated to the purpose of a bleeder resistor.

G7A02.hint: The filter part of a power supply smooths ripple using energy-storage parts: capacitors and often inductors.
G7A02.explanation: The correct answer is "Capacitors and inductors" because power-supply filter networks use these components to smooth rectified DC and reduce ripple.
why_wrong: Diodes do rectification, not the actual filtering stage named here.
Transformers may be part of the supply, but not the filter network itself; transducers are unrelated.
Because not all listed parts belong in the filter network, 'all of these' is wrong.

G7A03.hint: Two diodes plus a center-tapped transformer is the classic full-wave rectifier arrangement. Bridge rectifiers use four diodes instead.
G7A03.explanation: The correct answer is "Full-wave" because a center-tapped transformer with two diodes rectifies both halves of the AC cycle, producing full-wave rectification.
G7A03.mnemonic: Center tap plus two diodes = full-wave.
why_wrong: A bridge rectifier is full-wave too, but it normally uses four diodes rather than two with a center tap.
Half-wave would use only one half-cycle.
Synchronous is a different rectifier style and not the classic center-tapped two-diode circuit.

G7A04.hint: The simplest rectifier is half-wave, and its signature feature is that it needs only one diode.
G7A04.explanation: The correct answer is "Only one diode is required" because a half-wave rectifier uses a single diode to pass one half of the AC cycle and block the other half.
why_wrong: Half-wave ripple frequency is lower than full-wave, not twice it.
Half-wave generally delivers less effective output than full-wave, not more.
The output is not two times peak input voltage.

G7A05.hint: Half-wave rectification uses only one half of the AC cycle, which is 180 degrees out of 360.
G7A05.explanation: The correct answer is "180 degrees" because a half-wave rectifier passes one half-cycle and blocks the other half-cycle.
why_wrong: 90 degrees is only one quarter of the cycle.
270 degrees is not one half of the cycle.
360 degrees would mean the entire AC cycle.

G7A06.hint: Full-wave rectification uses both halves of the AC waveform, so all 360 degrees contribute to the DC output.
G7A06.explanation: The correct answer is "360 degrees" because a full-wave rectifier inverts the negative half-cycle so both halves become output pulses.
why_wrong: 90 degrees is only a quarter-cycle.
180 degrees describes half-wave, not full-wave, rectification.
270 degrees is still not the whole cycle.

G7A07.hint: An unfiltered full-wave rectifier does not make smooth DC yet. It makes pulsating DC, and because both halves are used, the pulse rate is twice line frequency.
G7A07.explanation: The correct answer is "A series of DC pulses at twice the frequency of the AC input" because each half-cycle contributes one pulse after full-wave rectification.
why_wrong: Same-frequency pulses would describe half-wave behavior, not full-wave.
The output is rectified pulses, not a lower-frequency sine wave.
Without filtering, the output is not steady DC.

G7A08.hint: Switchmode supplies operate at high frequency, which lets transformers and filters be much smaller than in linear supplies.
G7A08.explanation: The correct answer is "High-frequency operation allows the use of smaller components" because higher switching frequency reduces the size needed for magnetic and filtering components.
why_wrong: Higher output voltage is not the main defining advantage here.
Switchmode supplies do not necessarily use fewer components.
They are not inherently more stable just because they are switchmode designs.

G7A09.hint: For the figure questions, use visual recognition first. The FET is the symbol with a gate controlling a channel rather than the three-lead bipolar transistor arrow style. In Figure G7-1 that is Symbol 1.
G7A09.explanation: The correct answer is "Symbol 1" because Figure G7-1 labels the field effect transistor with that symbol number.
why_wrong: That symbol is not the FET in Figure G7-1.
That choice also points to the wrong figure symbol.
This symbol number does not correspond to the FET.

G7A10.hint: A Zener diode looks like a diode symbol with the special bent-bar cathode style. In Figure G7-1 that is Symbol 5.
G7A10.explanation: The correct answer is "Symbol 5" because Figure G7-1 labels the Zener diode with that symbol number.
why_wrong: That figure symbol is not the Zener diode.
This one is also not the Zener symbol.
Symbol 11 identifies something else in the figure.

G7A11.hint: For an NPN transistor, remember the emitter arrow points out. In Figure G7-1, the NPN BJT is Symbol 2.
G7A11.explanation: The correct answer is "Symbol 2" because Figure G7-1 identifies the NPN junction transistor with that number.
why_wrong: Symbol 1 is the FET in this figure, not the NPN transistor.
Symbol 7 is not the NPN transistor.
Symbol 11 is not the NPN transistor either.

G7A12.hint: A solid-core transformer symbol is the transformer with core lines shown between the windings. In Figure G7-1 that is Symbol 6.
G7A12.explanation: The correct answer is "Symbol 6" because Figure G7-1 labels the solid-core transformer with that number.
G7A12.mnemonic: Core lines mean transformer core, Symbol 6.
why_wrong: That symbol is not the solid-core transformer in the figure.
Symbol 7 is the tapped inductor, not the solid-core transformer.
Symbol 1 is the FET, not a transformer.

G7A13.hint: A tapped inductor is the coil symbol with an extra connection partway along the winding. In Figure G7-1 that is Symbol 7.
G7A13.explanation: The correct answer is "Symbol 7" because Figure G7-1 labels the tapped inductor with that number.
why_wrong: Symbol 11 is not the tapped inductor.
Symbol 6 is the solid-core transformer instead.
Symbol 1 is the FET, not an inductor.

G7B01.hint: Neutralization in an amplifier is about canceling unwanted internal feedback so the stage does not break into oscillation on its own.
G7B01.explanation: The correct answer is "To eliminate self-oscillations" because neutralization is used to counteract stray feedback within an amplifier stage. That helps prevent the amplifier from becoming unstable and oscillating.
why_wrong: Neutralization is not a modulation-index control technique.
Standby cutoff is a different control function.
Carrier frequency stability is not the main reason an amplifier is neutralized.

G7B02.hint: Among the common amplifier classes listed, Class C is the most efficient because it conducts for less than half the cycle.
G7B02.explanation: The correct answer is "Class C" because Class C operation sacrifices linearity for high efficiency by conducting for less than 180 degrees of the waveform.
G7B02.mnemonic: Class C = highest efficiency, lowest linearity.
why_wrong: Class A is the least efficient of the common amplifier classes here.
Class B is more efficient than A, but not as efficient as Class C.
Class AB trades some efficiency for better linearity, so it still does not beat Class C.

G7B03.hint: AND means all required conditions must be true. For a two-input AND gate, both inputs must be high before the output goes high.
G7B03.explanation: The correct answer is "Output is high only when both inputs are high" because the AND gate produces a logic 1 only when every required input is logic 1.
G7B03.mnemonic: AND = all need to be high.
why_wrong: This does not describe an AND truth table.
This is also not the behavior of a standard AND gate.
That is effectively the opposite of the correct AND condition.

G7B04.hint: Class A means the active device conducts through the entire waveform cycle. Entire cycle means 100 percent.
G7B04.explanation: The correct answer is "100%" because in Class A operation the amplifying device stays conducting for the full 360 degrees of the signal cycle.
why_wrong: More than 50 percent but less than 100 percent fits Class AB, not Class A.
50 percent fits Class B behavior.
Less than 50 percent is closer to Class C behavior.

G7B05.hint: A binary counter with `n` bits has `2^n` states. With 3 bits, that is `2^3 = 8`.
G7B05.explanation: The correct answer is "8" because each bit doubles the number of possible states. Three bits therefore produce eight distinct count states.
why_wrong: 3 would be counting bits, not states.
6 is not a power-of-two result for 3 bits.
16 would require 4 bits, not 3.

G7B06.hint: A shift register moves digital data from stage to stage on clock pulses. Think of data being shifted along a row of storage elements.
G7B06.explanation: The correct answer is "A clocked array of circuits that passes data in steps along the array" because shift registers store and move data one step at a time in sync with a clock signal.
why_wrong: Operational amplifiers are not what defines a shift register.
A shift register is a data-storage and transfer device, not a mixer.
It is also not an analog mixer.

G7B07.hint: A sine wave oscillator needs gain and frequency selection, tied together by feedback. That points to an amplifier plus a frequency-selective network in a feedback loop.
G7B07.explanation: The correct answer is "A filter and an amplifier operating in a feedback loop" because oscillators rely on amplification plus feedback through a frequency-determining network to sustain a sine wave.
why_wrong: A divider does not provide the frequency-selective feedback needed for sine-wave generation.
A multiplier and mixer are different signal-processing blocks, not the basic oscillator pair.
Feed-forward is not the sustaining-feedback arrangement a sine-wave oscillator needs.

G7B08.hint: Efficiency is useful output divided by input. For an RF power amplifier, that means RF output power divided by DC input power.
G7B08.explanation: The correct answer is "Divide the RF output power by the DC input power" because amplifier efficiency compares how much RF power is delivered to the load versus how much DC power the amplifier consumes.
why_wrong: This inverts the relationship and does not give efficiency.
This is not the standard efficiency calculation.
Adding unlike power terms is not how efficiency is defined.

G7B09.hint: An LC oscillator gets its frequency from the tuned tank circuit. The L and C values set the resonance.
G7B09.explanation: The correct answer is "The inductance and capacitance in the tank circuit" because the resonant frequency of an LC oscillator is determined by those two component values.
why_wrong: Counter stages are part of digital counting, not LC resonance.
Divider stages may create derived frequencies in other circuits, but not the basic LC oscillator frequency here.
Lag-circuit time delay is not the tuned-circuit principle being asked about.

G7B10.hint: A linear amplifier makes the signal bigger without reshaping its modulation. Preserve the waveform is the key phrase.
G7B10.explanation: The correct answer is "An amplifier in which the output preserves the input waveform" because linear amplification means the output is a scaled version of the input without intentional distortion of its shape.
G7B10.mnemonic: Linear means shape stays the same.
why_wrong: Not every RF power amplifier is linear.
Class C prioritizes efficiency over waveform preservation and is not linear for modulated signals.
A frequency multiplier intentionally changes the waveform and frequency, so it is not the definition of a linear amplifier.

G7B11.hint: Class C is non-linear, so it works for constant-envelope modes like FM, but not for amplitude-varying modes like AM or SSB.
G7B11.explanation: The correct answer is "FM" because FM conveys information by frequency variation while keeping amplitude essentially constant. That makes it suitable for a non-linear but efficient Class C power stage.
why_wrong: SSB needs linear amplification because the amplitude envelope carries information.
AM also depends on amplitude information and therefore is not appropriate for Class C amplification.
Because SSB and AM are not suitable here, 'all of these' cannot be correct.

G7C01.hint: A balanced modulator creates double sideband. To keep only one sideband for SSB, the next stage must remove the unwanted sideband with a filter.
G7C01.explanation: The correct answer is "Filter" because after a balanced modulator produces double-sideband suppressed-carrier output, a sideband-selecting filter is used to pass only the desired sideband and reject the other.
G7C01.mnemonic: Balanced modulator makes two; filter picks one.
why_wrong: A carrier oscillator provides a signal source, but it does not select one sideband from the two produced.
An IF amplifier boosts signals; it does not by itself perform the sideband-selection job asked here.
An RF amplifier also amplifies rather than selecting one sideband.

G7C02.hint: A balanced modulator suppresses the carrier but produces both sidebands. The key output idea is double-sideband RF.
G7C02.explanation: The correct answer is "Double-sideband modulated RF" because a balanced modulator combines audio and RF in a way that generates both sidebands while greatly reducing or suppressing the carrier.
G7C02.mnemonic: Balanced modulator: both sidebands, little carrier.
why_wrong: Balanced modulators are not FM generators in this context.
The output is RF, not equalized audio.
Extracted audio is something a detector would produce, not a modulator.

G7C03.hint: An impedance matching transformer is there to make each side see the impedance it wants. Think 'make the transmitter and feed line happy at the same time.'
G7C03.explanation: The correct answer is "To present the desired impedance to the transmitter and feed line" because matching transformers transform one impedance value into another so connected equipment sees a proper load.
G7C03.mnemonic: Matching transformer matches what each side sees.
why_wrong: Impedance matching is normally used to improve power transfer, not minimize transmitter power output.
Power-supply ripple is unrelated to a transmitter-output matching transformer.
Radiation resistance is an antenna property, not the point of this output transformer question.

G7C04.hint: A product detector mixes the incoming SSB signal with a local oscillator to recover the audio. That is why it belongs in an SSB receiver.
G7C04.explanation: The correct answer is "Used in a single sideband receiver to extract the modulated signal" because a product detector combines the received signal with a beat-frequency oscillator or carrier reinsertion signal to recover the audio information.
G7C04.mnemonic: Product detector produces audio from SSB.
why_wrong: That is not the normal ham-radio use being tested here.
A product detector is mainly a receiver function, not a transmitter multiplier stage.
FM receivers do not use a product detector to filter sidebands this way.

G7C05.hint: DDS is popular because it gives tuning flexibility while staying locked to a stable reference. The key pairing is variable frequency plus crystal-like stability.
G7C05.explanation: The correct answer is "Variable output frequency with the stability of a crystal oscillator" because a direct digital synthesizer derives output frequencies from a stable reference clock while allowing precise digital frequency control.
why_wrong: DDS units are not defined by an extremely narrow tuning range; in fact, flexibility is a major advantage.
DDS is usually a signal-generation technique, not a high-power output stage.
Pure sine-wave output is not the defining characteristic in this question.

G7C06.hint: DSP filters are flexible in a way fixed analog filters are not. Software can create many different bandwidths and shapes from the same hardware.
G7C06.explanation: The correct answer is "A wide range of filter bandwidths and shapes can be created" because digital signal processing can implement many selectable filter responses without changing physical components.
why_wrong: DSP does not inherently require fewer digital components just because it is digital.
Mixing-product reduction is not the main comparison point being asked here.
DSP filtering is not defined by being much more effective specifically at VHF.

G7C07.hint: Loss inside the passband is called insertion loss. The key is that the signal is in the passband, but still gets reduced some.
G7C07.explanation: The correct answer is "Insertion loss" because insertion loss describes how much desired signal is lost while passing through the filter's intended passband.
why_wrong: Return loss refers to mismatch and reflected power, not passband attenuation.
Q is a selectivity-related parameter, not the name for passband attenuation.
Ultimate rejection refers to stopband rejection, not passband loss.

G7C08.hint: Receiver sensitivity depends on multiple stages and tradeoffs, including front-end gain, demodulator bandwidth, and especially noise figure. When several of those are listed, expect 'all of these.'
G7C08.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because receiver sensitivity is influenced by input amplifier gain, demodulator bandwidth, and input amplifier noise figure among other factors.
G7C08.mnemonic: Sensitivity is a chain problem, not a one-part problem.
why_wrong: Front-end gain matters, but it is not the only sensitivity factor listed.
Bandwidth affects how much noise is admitted, so this also matters but is not the whole answer.
Noise figure is crucial too, which is why the full answer includes all the listed factors.

G7C09.hint: I and Q mean in-phase and quadrature. Quadrature signals are separated by 90 degrees.
G7C09.explanation: The correct answer is "90 degrees" because SDR I and Q channels are orthogonal components of the signal, and orthogonal phase separation here means 90 degrees.
why_wrong: Zero degrees would make the signals identical rather than quadrature components.
180 degrees is opposite phase, not quadrature.
45 degrees is not the standard I-Q phase separation.

G7C10.hint: I-Q modulation is powerful because software can shape and combine I and Q to synthesize many modulation types. Flexibility is the big advantage.
G7C10.explanation: The correct answer is "All types of modulation can be created with appropriate processing" because by processing the in-phase and quadrature components digitally, SDRs can generate many different modulation schemes.
why_wrong: High-resolution converters are often still very important in SDR design.
Minimum detectable signal is not automatically reduced just because I-Q modulation is used.
Digital-to-analog conversion is not automatic in the way this choice suggests.

G7C11.hint: In an SDR, software often takes over jobs that used to be done by dedicated hardware blocks. Filtering, detection, and modulation can all be software functions.
G7C11.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because software in an SDR can perform filtering, detection, and modulation functions depending on the design.
G7C11.mnemonic: SDR moves many radio jobs into software.
why_wrong: Filtering is one software-performed SDR function, but not the only one listed.
Detection is also often done in software, but it is still only part of the answer.
Modulation can be software-based too, which is why the complete answer is all of them.

G7C12.hint: The point where a low-pass filter has dropped to less than half the input power is the cutoff frequency, often associated with the 3 dB point.
G7C12.explanation: The correct answer is "Cutoff frequency" because that term names the boundary where the filter begins significant attenuation and output power has fallen below half power.
why_wrong: Notch frequency refers to a deep rejection point, not the low-pass half-power boundary.
Neper is a logarithmic unit, not the term used here.
Rolloff describes how fast attenuation increases, not the specific half-power boundary frequency.

G7C13.hint: Maximum stopband rejection is called ultimate rejection. Think 'how well can the filter reject unwanted signals at its best.'
G7C13.explanation: The correct answer is "Ultimate rejection" because this term specifies the greatest attenuation the filter can provide outside the passband.
why_wrong: Notch depth is about a specific notch response, not the overall maximum stopband rejection term here.
Rolloff describes the slope of attenuation, not the maximum rejection value.
Insertion loss refers to passband loss, not stopband rejection.

G7C14.hint: Band-pass filter bandwidth is measured between the two half-power points, one on each side of center frequency.
G7C14.explanation: The correct answer is "Upper and lower half-power" because bandwidth is typically defined between the frequencies where the response has fallen to half power, also known as the 3 dB points.
G7C14.mnemonic: Bandwidth lives between the two 3 dB points.
why_wrong: Cutoff and rolloff are different filter descriptors, not the two frequencies used for bandwidth measurement here.
Pole and zero are analysis terms, not the usual bandwidth measurement pair.
Image and harmonic are unrelated to the definition of band-pass filter bandwidth.

G8A01.hint: Direct binary FSK literally switches the oscillator between frequencies under digital control. The key idea is direct frequency changing, not audio tricks.
G8A01.explanation: The correct answer is "By changing an oscillator’s frequency directly with a digital control signal" because FSK conveys data by shifting between discrete frequencies, and direct binary FSK does that at the oscillator itself.
why_wrong: A sub-audible tone is not how direct binary FSK is generated in this context.
Computer control may set frequencies overall, but that is not the modulation mechanism being asked about.
CW keying input repurposing is not the definition of direct binary FSK.

G8A02.hint: If the question says the RF phase angle is changed to carry information, that is phase modulation by definition.
G8A02.explanation: The correct answer is "Phase modulation" because PM encodes information by varying the phase of the RF signal.
why_wrong: Convolution is a signal-processing math operation, not this modulation type.
Transformation is too generic and not the standard modulation term.
Inversion is not the term for encoding information by changing phase angle.

G8A03.hint: If information is carried by changing the instantaneous frequency, that is FM directly and literally.
G8A03.explanation: The correct answer is "Frequency modulation" because FM conveys information by varying the instantaneous frequency of the carrier.
why_wrong: Convolution is not a modulation mode here.
Transformation is not the established term for this process.
Frequency conversion means moving a signal to another frequency, not modulating it with information.

G8A04.hint: A reactance modulator changes the phase relationship in the RF stage, producing phase modulation.
G8A04.explanation: The correct answer is "Phase modulation" because a reactance modulator changes the effective reactance in the RF stage, which results in phase modulation behavior at that stage.
why_wrong: Multiplex modulation is not what a reactance modulator produces here.
The effect is not basic amplitude modulation.
It is also not pulse modulation.

G8A05.hint: When the instantaneous power or envelope level changes, you are dealing with amplitude modulation.
G8A05.explanation: The correct answer is "Amplitude modulation" because AM conveys information by varying the amplitude, and therefore the instantaneous power level, of the RF signal.
why_wrong: Power modulation is not the standard named emission mode here.
Phase modulation changes phase, not signal amplitude.
Frequency modulation changes frequency, not the RF power envelope.

G8A06.hint: QPSK31 keeps about the same bandwidth as BPSK31, adds coding advantages, and is sideband-sensitive like the other PSK modes. When all three seem true, they are.
G8A06.explanation: The correct answer is "All these choices are correct" because QPSK31 is sideband sensitive, uses encoding that provides error-correction benefits, and has bandwidth approximately the same as BPSK31.
G8A06.mnemonic: QPSK31 keeps the width and gains coding help.
why_wrong: This statement is true, but it is only one part of the full answer.
This is also true, but not the whole story.
This is true too, which is why the right answer is all of them.

G8A07.hint: Among phone emissions, SSB is the narrowest because it removes the carrier and one sideband, leaving only one sideband to transmit voice.
G8A07.explanation: The correct answer is "Single sideband" because SSB is spectrally efficient and uses less bandwidth than the other listed phone modes.
why_wrong: Vestigial sideband is not typically narrower than SSB.
Phase-modulated phone usually occupies more bandwidth than SSB voice.
FM phone is one of the wider phone emissions listed.

G8A08.hint: Overmodulation splatters. When you drive modulation too hard, the occupied bandwidth spreads wider than it should.
G8A08.explanation: The correct answer is "Excessive bandwidth" because overmodulation creates distortion products and spectral splatter outside the intended channel width.
why_wrong: Overmodulation is not caused by too little audio.
Insufficient bandwidth is the opposite of the normal consequence here.
Frequency drift is a separate stability problem, not the classic overmodulation symptom.

G8A09.hint: FT8 is an 8-tone digital mode. The '8' in FT8 pairs well with remembering 8-tone FSK.
G8A09.explanation: The correct answer is "8-tone frequency shift keying" because FT8 transmits digital information using eight discrete audio tones in an FSK-style scheme.
why_wrong: Vestigial sideband is a different modulation family entirely.
Amplitude-compressed AM is not the FT8 mode type.
Direct-sequence spread spectrum is also not what FT8 uses.

G8A10.hint: Flat-topping means the peaks are getting clipped because the transmitter is being driven too hard. Excess drive flattens the top of the envelope.
G8A10.explanation: The correct answer is "Signal distortion caused by excessive drive or speech levels" because excessive audio or RF drive pushes the transmitter into clipping, flattening the tops of the modulation envelope peaks.
G8A10.mnemonic: Too much drive flattens the peaks.
why_wrong: Insufficient collector current is not the standard definition of flat-topping here.
Properly adjusted ALC should help avoid this problem, not define it.
Carrier suppression is an SSB issue, not the definition of flat-topping.

G8A11.hint: The AM envelope is the outer outline of the modulated RF waveform. If you connect the tops of the peaks, you trace the envelope.
G8A11.explanation: The correct answer is "The waveform created by connecting the peak values of the modulated signal" because the envelope is the smooth outline formed by the RF peaks as amplitude varies with the modulation.
why_wrong: The carrier frequency is part of the signal, but not the definition of the envelope.
Spurious signals are not what the envelope refers to.
Bandwidth is a spectral measure, not the time-domain envelope shape.

G8A12.hint: QPSK uses four phase states, and four states can represent pairs of bits. The four remembered phases are 0, 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
G8A12.explanation: The correct answer is "Modulation in which digital data is transmitted using 0-, 90-, 180- and 270-degrees phase shift to represent pairs of bits" because quadrature phase-shift keying uses four discrete phase states, allowing two bits per symbol.
G8A12.mnemonic: QPSK = four phases for two-bit symbols.
why_wrong: This is not what the QP in QPSK stands for.
Quadra-pole sideband keying is not the real expansion or concept.
Fast Fourier Transforms are not the defining mechanism of QPSK modulation.

G8A13.hint: A link budget is the full RF accounting sheet: start with transmit power, add gains, subtract losses, and see what arrives at the receiver.
G8A13.explanation: The correct answer is "The sum of transmit power and antenna gains minus system losses as seen at the receiver" because link budgets combine gains and losses across the path to estimate received signal level.
G8A13.mnemonic: Link budget balances gains and losses.
why_wrong: This is a radio-engineering budget, not a money budget.
Antenna gains alone minus losses leaves out transmit power and is incomplete.
Receiver sensitivity matters in margin calculations, but the link budget itself is the full path accounting before that comparison.

G8A14.hint: Link margin is the safety cushion. It is how far above the minimum required signal level your actual received signal sits.
G8A14.explanation: The correct answer is "The difference between received power level and minimum required signal level at the input to the receiver" because link margin measures how much signal headroom the link has beyond the minimum needed for reliable reception.
why_wrong: Fade margin and link margin are related ideas, not simple opposites.
Transmit power minus receiver sensitivity ignores path gains and losses and is too incomplete.
Receiver sensitivity plus 3 dB is just an arbitrary offset, not the definition of link margin.

G8B01.hint: In a mixer, the tuned or varied source is the local oscillator. You keep the IF fixed by changing the LO as the incoming signal frequency changes.
G8B01.explanation: The correct answer is "Local oscillator" because the LO is adjusted so that, when mixed with the incoming RF, the desired intermediate frequency is produced.
G8B01.mnemonic: Tune the LO to keep the IF fixed.
why_wrong: Image frequency is an unwanted response, not the mixer input you intentionally tune.
The RF input is the received signal itself, not the variable source used to set conversion frequency.
A beat-frequency oscillator is used in specific detector stages, not as the main tuned mixer input in this question.

G8B02.hint: A signal spaced from the desired one by twice the IF can also convert to the same IF. That classic unwanted mixer effect is the image response.
G8B02.explanation: The correct answer is "Image response" because a superheterodyne mixer can respond to an undesired signal offset by twice the IF from the desired frequency, producing the same IF output.
why_wrong: Quadrature refers to 90-degree phase relationships, not this receiver interference effect.
Mixer interference is too vague and not the standard term.
Intermediate interference is also not the established receiver term for this phenomenon.

G8B03.hint: Mixing two RF signals to get new frequencies is called heterodyning. Sum-and-difference products are the clue.
G8B03.explanation: The correct answer is "Heterodyning" because that is the standard term for mixing two signals in a nonlinear device to create sum and difference frequencies.
why_wrong: Synthesizing is a broader frequency-generation idea, not the specific term for mixing two RF signals.
Frequency inversion can happen in conversion systems, but it is not the general name for mixing itself.
Phase inversion is unrelated to this mixer action.

G8B04.hint: To reach a higher transmit frequency from a lower-frequency source by using harmonics, you use a frequency multiplier.
G8B04.explanation: The correct answer is "Multiplier" because a frequency multiplier uses harmonic generation to produce a higher operating frequency from a lower-frequency signal source.
G8B04.mnemonic: Harmonics to higher frequency means multiplier.
why_wrong: A mixer combines two different frequencies; it is not the usual harmonic-generator stage described here.
A reactance modulator changes phase or frequency characteristics, not the frequency by harmonic multiplication.
Balanced converter is not the standard term for this stage.

G8B05.hint: The intermod products that land closest to the original signals are usually the odd-order ones, especially third-order products.
G8B05.explanation: The correct answer is "Odd-order" because odd-order intermodulation products, such as third-order products, tend to appear closest to the desired signal frequencies and are therefore especially troublesome.
G8B05.mnemonic: Odd-order IMD crowds the wanted signals.
why_wrong: Second harmonics are harmonics, not the closest intermod products referred to here.
Even-order products are generally not the closest troublesome pair around the original signals.
Intercept point is a performance metric, not a product type.

G8B06.hint: Use Carson's Rule: bandwidth is about `2 x (deviation + highest modulating frequency)`. Here that is `2 x (5 + 3) = 16 kHz`.
G8B06.explanation: The correct answer is "16 kHz" because an FM phone signal with 5 kHz deviation and 3 kHz modulating frequency has an approximate bandwidth of 2(5 + 3) kHz.
G8B06.mnemonic: Carson: two times deviation plus audio.
why_wrong: 3 kHz counts only the audio, not the FM bandwidth.
5 kHz counts only the deviation, not the total occupied bandwidth.
8 kHz is only deviation plus audio once; Carson's Rule doubles that sum.

G8B07.hint: If the final transmitter multiplies the oscillator frequency by 12, it also multiplies deviation by 12. So the oscillator deviation must be `5000 / 12 = 416.7 Hz`.
G8B07.explanation: The correct answer is "416.7 Hz" because 146.52 MHz is 12 times 12.21 MHz. Since frequency multipliers also multiply deviation, the oscillator deviation must be the final 5 kHz divided by 12.
G8B07.mnemonic: Divide deviation by the multiplication factor.
why_wrong: 101.75 Hz is too small for a 12-times multiplier relationship.
5 kHz is the final transmitter deviation, not the oscillator deviation before multiplication.
60 kHz is what you might get by multiplying in the wrong direction.

G8B08.hint: Duty cycle matters because some digital or carrier-heavy modes keep the transmitter working hard almost continuously, increasing heat and average-power stress.
G8B08.explanation: The correct answer is "Some modes have high duty cycles that could exceed the transmitter’s average power rating" because high-duty-cycle modes can overheat equipment or exceed average-power design limits even if peak power seems acceptable.
G8B08.mnemonic: High duty cycle means high heat risk.
why_wrong: Duty cycle is not mainly a tuning aid.
Break-in timing is a separate operating issue.
Overmodulation is about modulation level, not duty cycle.

G8B09.hint: A receiver bandwidth that matches the mode lets in enough signal but not extra noise. That is how you get the best SNR.
G8B09.explanation: The correct answer is "It results in the best signal-to-noise ratio" because overly wide bandwidth admits unnecessary noise, while a matched bandwidth preserves the signal and rejects excess noise.
G8B09.mnemonic: Match the bandwidth, improve the SNR.
why_wrong: This is an operating-performance issue, not an FCC rule requirement.
Receiver power consumption is not the main reason.
Bandwidth choice does not improve antenna impedance matching.

G8B10.hint: Faster symbol changes require more spectrum. Higher symbol rate generally means wider occupied bandwidth.
G8B10.explanation: The correct answer is "Higher symbol rates require wider bandwidth" because increasing the symbol rate increases how rapidly the waveform changes, which broadens the required spectrum.
why_wrong: Symbol rate and bandwidth are definitely related.
Lower symbol rates generally need less, not more, bandwidth.
Bandwidth is not fixed at one-half the symbol rate as a universal rule.

G8B11.hint: A mixer creates sum and difference products from its two inputs. That is the classic mixer outcome to remember.
G8B11.explanation: The correct answer is "The sum and difference" because nonlinear mixing combines the LO and RF inputs to produce output components at both the sum and the difference of their frequencies.
G8B11.mnemonic: Mixers make add and subtract frequencies.
why_wrong: A ratio is not a normal mixer output product.
An average is not the standard result of RF mixing.
The arithmetic product is not how mixer outputs are described.

G8B12.hint: Unwanted spurious outputs from combining signals in a nonlinear circuit are intermodulation products. Nonlinearity is the key clue.
